name: kettlebell bottoms up clean from the hang position
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in one hand with an overhand grip.
Bend your knees slightly and hinge forward at the hips, keeping your back straight and chest up.
Allow the kettlebell to hang down between your legs, with your arm fully extended.
In one fluid motion, explosively extend your hips and knees while pulling the kettlebell up towards your shoulder.
As the kettlebell reaches shoulder height, rotate your wrist so that the bottom of the kettlebell is facing up.
Catch the kettlebell at shoulder height with your elbow bent and your palm facing up.
Lower the kettlebell back down to the starting position by reversing the movement.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch sides.

Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in one hand with an overhand grip. Bend your knees slightly and hinge forward at the hips, keeping your back straight and chest up. Allow the kettlebell to hang down between your legs, with your arm fully extended. In one fluid motion, explosively extend your hips and knees while pulling the kettlebell up towards your shoulder. As the kettlebell reaches shoulder height, rotate your wrist so that the bottom of the kettlebell is facing up. Catch the kettlebell at shoulder height with your elbow bent and your palm facing up. Lower the kettlebell back down to the starting position by reversing the movement. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch sides.

conditionNotes.note: Keep load symmetrical and stop at positions that create sharp pain or twisting.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Maintain neutral spine and avoid positions that aggravate the lower back.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
